message.content:
Search the web for papers that use wavelet-based attention or frequency-domain attention specifically for steel surface defect detection with YOLO models. I need to verify if this combination is novel or if someone has already published it.

Search for:
1. "wavelet attention steel defect detection"
2. "frequency domain attention YOLO steel"
3. "DWT attention steel surface defect"
4. "wavelet YOLO defect detection 2025 2026"
5. "frequency channel attention steel inspection"

For each result found, report:
- Paper title
- Year
- What they do (wavelet/frequency + attention + steel?)
- How close it is to: "a module that applies 2D DWT to decompose YOLO feature maps into frequency subbands, applies per-subband channel attention, then reconstructs via inverse DWT for steel defect detection"

Be thorough. I need to know if this specific combination exists in the literature. Report in under 300 words.

- brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- firecrawl: Web scraping, search, crawling, and page interaction via the Firecrawl CLI. Use this skill whenever the user wants to search the web, find articles, research a topic, look something up online, scrape a webpage, grab content from a URL, extract data from a website, crawl documentation, download a site, or interact with pages that need clicks or logins. Also use when they say "fetch this page", "pull the content from", "get the page at https://", or reference scraping external websites. This provides real-time web search with full page content extraction and interact capabilities — beyond what Claude can do natively with built-in tools. Do NOT trigger for local file operations, git commands, deployments, or code editing tasks.
- firecrawl-agent: AI-powered autonomous data extraction that navigates complex sites and returns structured JSON. Use this skill when the user wants structured data from websites, needs to extract pricing tiers, product listings, directory entries, or any data as JSON with a schema. Triggers on "extract structured data", "get all the products", "pull pricing info", "extract as JSON", or when the user provides a JSON schema for website data. More powerful than simple scraping for multi-page structured extraction.
- firecrawl-browser: DEPRECATED — use scrape + interact instead. Interact lets you scrape a page and then click, fill forms, and navigate without managing sessions manually. Use this skill when the user needs to interact with a webpage, log into a site, click buttons, fill forms, navigate multi-step flows, handle pagination, or when regular scraping fails because content requires JavaScript interaction. Triggers on "click", "fill out the form", "log in to", "paginated", "infinite scroll", "interact with the page", or "scrape failed".

Use this skill when the user wants to crawl a site, extract all pages from a docs section, bulk-scrape multiple pages following links, or says "crawl", "get all the pages", "extract everything under /docs", "bulk extract", or needs content from many pages on the same site. Handles depth limits, path filtering, and concurrent extraction.
- firecrawl-download: Download an entire website as local files — markdown, screenshots, or multiple formats per page. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). content.content:
Web search results for query: "frequency domain attention YOLO steel surface defect"

Links: [{"title":"HDFA-Net: A high-dimensional decoupled frequency attention network for steel surface defect detection - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263224124021407"},{"title":"Detection of Surface Defects in Steel Based on Dual-Backbone Network: MBDNet-Attention-YOLO","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12349437/"},{"title":"Research on steel surface defect classification method based on deep learning","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11001973/"},{"title":"YOLOv11-WBD: A wavelet-bidirectional network with dilated perception for robust metal surface defect detection","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12422516/"},{"title":"HDSA-YOLO: a steel surface defect detection network via multi-scale haar wavelet fusion and deformable-swin integration","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/398267055_HDSA-YOLO_a_steel_surface_defect_detection_network_via_multi-scale_haar_wavelet_fusion_and_deformable-swin_integration"},{"title":"An Optimized YOLOv8 Framework for Automatic Manufacturing Defect Detection in Bayonet-Bulb Base Holders | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-032-19678-1_47"},{"title":"MWYOLO: A Mamba-Enhanced Lightweight YOLO Framework with Multi-Frequency Attention for Industrial Surface Defect Detection","url":"https://www.mdpi.com/3042-8831/1/1/3"}]

Here are the search results for **"frequency domain attention YOLO steel surface defect"**:

---

## Key Research Papers Found

### 1. HDFA-Net: High-Dimensional Decoupled Frequency Attention Network
*(ScienceDirect, November 2024)*

To address the challenge of low detection accuracy caused by diverse defect types and complex backgrounds in industrial surface defect detection, the YOLO hourglass-based hybrid attention mechanism and multiscale feature fusion (HAMF-YOLOH) model is proposed. The shallow and deep features are extracted by the backbone network and put into the frequency and dilated attention mechanism module to enhance the extraction of key feature information and suppress redundant information interference.

### 2. SFC-YOLOv8: Spatial-Frequency Domain-Optimized YOLOv8
*(IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement, 2025)*

Liu, H., Hu, R., Dong, H., Liu, Z. proposed SFC-YOLOv8: enhanced strip steel surface defect detection using spatial-frequency domain-optimized YOLOv8.

### 3. YOLOv11-WBD: Wavelet-Bidirectional Network with Dilated Perception
*(NCBI/PMC)*

Metal surface images under operational conditions often exhibit coexisting high-frequency noise components and spectral aliasing background textures, and defect targets typically exhibit characteristics such as small scale, weak contrast, and multi-class coexistence. To address this, concepts including wavelet decomposition, cross-attention, and U-shaped dilated convolution are introduced into the YOLO framework, proposing the YOLOv11-WBD model. A plug-and-play Wavelet-Attentive Multiband Fusion Module (WAMF) is designed, achieving decoupling of low-frequency and high-frequency features and adaptive multi-frequency fusion.

### 4. HDSA-YOLO: Haar Wavelet Fusion and Deformable-Swin Integration
*(ResearchGate, December 2025)*

Steel surface defect detection is critical for industrial inspection but remains challenging due to irregular shapes, scale variations, low contrast and high noise. The Dual-Channel Attention Block (DCAB) jointly models channel and spatial dependencies to emphasize defect-relevant features.

### 5. MWYOLO: Mamba-Enhanced YOLO with Multi-Frequency Attention
*(MDPI, ~3 weeks ago)*

Industrial surface defect detection remains challenging due to complex textures, diverse defect scales, and stringent real-time constraints. MWYOLO is an enhanced YOLO11-based detection framework tailored for accurate and efficient industrial inspection. A multi-scale wavelet attention (MWA) module is embedded into the backbone, leveraging wavelet-domain feature decomposition and dual attention to capture multi-frequency patterns, thereby improving sensitivity to fine-grained and subtle defect patterns.

### 6. MBDNet-Attention-YOLO (MBY)
*(NCBI/PMC)*

Automated surface defect detection in steel manufacturing is pivotal for ensuring product quality, yet it remains an open challenge owing to the extreme heterogeneity of defect morphologies. MBY (MBDNet-Attention-YOLO) is a lightweight yet powerful framework that synergistically couples the MBDNet backbone with the YOLO detection head.

### 7. YOLOv5-KBS
*(NCBI/PMC)*

YOLOv5-KBS is a novel approach for surface flaw identification based on YOLOv5, integrating attention mechanism and weighted Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network (BiFPN) into YOLOv5 architecture. The YOLOv5-KBS model achieves a notable 4.2% increase in mean Average Precision (mAP) and reaches a detection speed of 70 FPS, outperforming the baseline model.

---

This is an active research area with many recent papers combining **frequency-domain analysis** (wavelet transforms, Fourier-based attention) with **YOLO-based detection frameworks** to improve steel surface defect detection accuracy and robustness.

Web search results for query: "frequency channel attention steel inspection deep learning"

Links: [{"title":"HDFA-Net: A high-dimensional decoupled frequency attention network for steel surface defect detection - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263224124021407"},{"title":"Improved faster R-CNN for steel surface defect detection in industrial quality control | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-12740-x"},{"title":"Quantitative Analysis of Metallographic Image Using Attention-Aware Deep Neural Networks","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7796035/"},{"title":"SH-DETR: Enhancing steel surface defect detection and classification with an improved transformer architecture | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0334048"},{"title":"Defect Detection in Steel Using a Hybrid Attention Network - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10422419/"},{"title":"Frontiers | Enhanced You Only Look Once X for surface defect detection of strip steel","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/neurorobotics/articles/10.3389/fnbot.2022.1042780/full"},{"title":"Deep Learning-Based Surface Defect Detection in Steel Products Using Convolutional Neural Networks | IIETA","url":"https://www.iieta.org/journals/mmep/paper/10.18280/mmep.111113"},{"title":"SH-DETR: Enhancing steel surface defect detection and classification with an improved transformer architecture","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12604762/"},{"title":"Enhanced You Only Look Once X for surface defect detection of strip steel","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9720119/"}]

Here are the search results for **"frequency channel attention steel inspection deep learning"**:

---

## Key Research Findings

### 1. HDFA-Net: High-Dimensional Decoupled Frequency Attention Network
*(ScienceDirect, November 2024)*

A novel HDFA mechanism computes the importance levels of features according to decomposed frequency cues, which is a more advantageous strategy for detecting the important features of subtle defects. As the method focuses on decoupling features directly in the frequency domain to learn important features, it can leverage high-frequency and low-frequency features to effectively extract fine-grained details and global cues for accurate detections. In addition, it considers frequency domain information at both channel and spatial levels. This attention mechanism is a plug-and-play module and can be embedded into other object detection frameworks to enhance their feature extraction capabilities.

### 2. Improved Faster R-CNN for Steel Surface Defect Detection
*(Nature Scientific Reports, 2025)*

This study builds upon the baseline Faster R-CNN framework, employing ResNet50-FPN as the feature extraction backbone while introducing an optimized feature fusion module and lightweight channel attention mechanism. The experimental findings validate the effectiveness of the "Feature Fusion + Attention Mechanism Faster R-CNN" architecture for steel defect identification, establishing a reliable theoretical foundation for automated surface defect inspection systems.

### 3. Hybrid Attention Network for Steel Defect Detection
*(PMC, 2023)*

Defect detection in steel surface focuses on accurately identifying and precisely locating defects on the surface of steel materials. Methods of defect detection with deep learning have gained significant attention in research. Existing algorithms can achieve satisfactory results, but the accuracy of defect detection still needs to be improved. Aiming at this issue, a hybrid attention network is proposed. A CBAM attention module is used to enhance the model's ability to learn effective features, and an adaptively spatial feature fusion (ASFF) module is used to improve the accuracy by extracting multi-scale information of defects.

### 4. SH-DETR: Transformer-Based Steel Defect Detection
*(PLOS One, November 2025)*

A deep learning framework that combines multi-channel random coding with modules for multi-scale feature fusion tackles the challenges of low recognition accuracy and insufficient classification power prevalent in conventional models. The model capitalizes on the self-attention mechanism associated with the Transformer architecture, alongside the strong feature extraction capabilities of CNNs.

### 5. Enhanced YOLOX for Strip Steel
*(Frontiers in Neurorobotics, 2022)*

A lightweight YOLOX surface defect detection network introduces the Multi-scale Feature Fusion Attention Module (MFFAM). MFFAM uses different scales of receptive fields for feature maps of different resolutions, after which features are fused and passed into the spatial and channel attention modules in parallel. The module significantly improves the accuracy of small-area defect detection.

### 6. Metallographic Image Analysis with Attention Networks
*(NCBI/PMC, 2021)*

Two attention-aware deep neural networks, MAUNet and SASAPD, are proposed to identify structures in metallographic steel images. MAUNet investigates the difference between low-frequency and high-frequency and prevents duplication of low-resolution information in skip connections, incorporating a spatial-channel attention module with the decoder to enhance interpretability of features.

---

## Summary

Research in this area is very active, with the **HDFA-Net** paper being the most directly relevant to frequency-based channel attention for steel inspection. It specifically decouples features in the frequency domain and computes channel/spatial importance from frequency cues. Other notable approaches combine channel attention mechanisms (CBAM, lightweight channel attention, spatial-channel parallel modules) with various deep learning architectures (YOLO variants, Faster R-CNN, DETR/Transformers) for automated steel surface defect detection.

Web search results for query: "wavelet attention steel defect detection"

Links: [{"title":"A novel steel surface defect detector with wavelet upsampling and frequency-domain attention: Nondestructive Testing and Evaluation: Vol 0, No 0 - Get Access","url":"https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10589759.2025.2566787"},{"title":"Wavelet Texture Descriptor for Steel Surface Defect Classification","url":"https://www.mdpi.com/1996-1944/17/23/5873"},{"title":"Intelligent Detection of Steel Defects Based on Improved Split Attention Networks - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8793735/"},{"title":"Wavelet Texture Descriptor for Steel Surface Defect Classification - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11642679/"},{"title":"Legendre multiwavelet-based feature attention guidance lightweight network for accurate steel surface defect classification - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0952197625021876"},{"title":"PeerJ Boosting Mamba-You Only Look Once (YOLO) with triple-aware enhancement for steel surface defect detection","url":"https://peerj.com/articles/cs-3626/"},{"title":"LWMS-Net: A novel defect detection network based on multi-wavelet multi-scale for steel surface defects - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263224125007523"},{"title":"YOLOv11-WBD: A wavelet-bidirectional network with dilated perception for robust metal surface defect detection","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12422516/"}]

Here are the results for your search on **"wavelet attention steel defect detection"**:

---

## Key Research Papers & Approaches

### 1. **Novel Steel Surface Defect Detector with Wavelet Upsampling and Frequency-Domain Attention**
*(Nondestructive Testing and Evaluation, 2025 — Taylor & Francis)*

This study proposes a steel surface defect detection model based on the real-time detection transformer, aimed at improving detection accuracy in complex industrial environments. Key contributions include:
- A dynamic scale aggregation module designed to fuse critical information from multiple pathways, enabling efficient multi-scale feature extraction.
- A frequency-enhanced dynamic attention mechanism utilizing the fast Fourier transform for global context modelling in the frequency domain, enhancing joint perception of low-frequency structural and high-frequency detailed features.
- A wavelet upsampling operator developed to decompose input features into high- and low-frequency components, enabling targeted refinement and adaptive fusion.

---

### 2. **LWFAG-LNet: Legendre Multiwavelet-Based Feature Attention Guidance Network**
*(ScienceDirect / Engineering Applications of AI)*

This paper combines Legendre multiwavelet (LW) with feature attention guidance (FAG) mechanism to devise a novel high-accuracy lightweight network (LWFAG-LNet) for surface defect classification. Specifically:
- LW bases with rich regularities are first utilized to match complex geometric characteristics across multi-wavelet and multi-scale resolution levels.
- The FAG module effectively fuses shallow high-resolution detailed features with deep low-resolution contextual features, significantly reducing the depth of the CNN.

---

### 3. **TriA-Mamba-YOLO: Triple-Aware Enhancement for Steel Defect Detection**
*(PeerJ Computer Science, March 2026)*

The authors propose TriA-Mamba-YOLO, a triple-awareness enhancement of the Mamba-YOLO framework for steel defect detection.
- Input samples are processed using a discrete wavelet transform, which reduces background noise and enhances the representation of defect features.
- The backbone is enhanced with a micro-feature-aware enhancement block comprising a Smooth-Mix Focus Stem block and a Selective Fusion Attention (SF-Att) block for selectively emphasizing discriminative micro-scale patterns.

---

### 4. **YOLOv11-WBD: Wavelet-Bidirectional Network with Dilated Perception**
*(NCBI/PMC, 2025)*

This work introduces concepts including wavelet decomposition, cross-attention, and U-shaped dilated convolution into the YOLO framework, proposing the YOLOv11-WBD model to enhance feature representation and semantic mining.
- A plug-and-play Wavelet-Attentive Multiband Fusion Module (WAMF) is designed, achieving decoupling of low-frequency and high-frequency features and adaptive multi-frequency fusion.

---

### 5. **Wavelet Texture Descriptor for Steel Surface Defect Classification**
*(MDPI Materials, November 2024)*

This paper proposes a novel method based on wavelet transform and texture descriptors for the robust and precise classification of steel surface defects.

---

### 6. **Intelligent Detection of Steel Defects Based on Improved Split Attention Networks**
*(PMC, 2022)*

The split attention network is an improvement of the residual network and the visual attention mechanism. This paper improves and optimises the network based on the feature pyramid network and split attention network in terms of data enhancement, multi-scale feature fusion, and network structure optimisation.

---

## Common Themes

These papers highlight that **combining wavelet transforms with attention mechanisms** is a powerful paradigm for steel defect detection because:
- Steel surface defect detection is a crucial step in improving product quality, ensuring safety, and enhancing production efficiency.
- Due to the multi-scale morphology, low contrast, and random positions of the defects, achieving a favorable balance between detection accuracy and speed remains challenging in practical applications.
- Wavelet decomposition helps separate frequency components, while attention mechanisms focus computational resources on the most informative features.

Web search results for query: "DWT attention steel surface defect detection"

Links: [{"title":"Detection and classification of surface defects on hot-rolled steel using vision transformers - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S240584402414529X"},{"title":"Detection and classification of surface defects on hot-rolled steel using vision transformers - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11490857/"},{"title":"Defect Detection in Steel Using a Hybrid Attention Network","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10422419/"},{"title":"PeerJ Boosting Mamba-You Only Look Once (YOLO) with triple-aware enhancement for steel surface defect detection","url":"https://peerj.com/articles/cs-3626/"},{"title":"Multi-Scale Deformable Transformer with Iterative Query Refinement for Hot-Rolled Steel Surface Defect Detection","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12656029/"},{"title":"A Steel Surface Defect Detection Method Based on Lightweight Convolution Optimization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.15476"},{"title":"Surface defect detection of hot rolled steel based on multi-scale feature fusion and attention mechanism residual block","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC10984981/"},{"title":"DSAT: a dynamic sparse attention transformer for steel surface defect detection with hierarchical feature fusion | Scientific Reports","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-14935-8"},{"title":"Hot rolled steel surface defect detection and classification using an automatic ensemble approach - IOPscience","url":"https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/2631-8695/ad4a26"}]

Here are the results for your search on **"DWT attention steel surface defect detection"**:

---

## Key Findings

### 1. DWT (Discrete Wavelet Transform) for Steel Surface Defect Detection

A recent study published on **PeerJ** (March 2026) proposes a Mamba-YOLO framework enhanced with wavelet transforms for steel defect detection. The researchers opted for wavelet transforms over other enhancement techniques because of their theoretical foundation in edge detection and localized frequency analysis. Since low-contrast and inconsistent textures can obstruct defect detection, the localization properties of wavelet transforms ensure that defect boundaries are precisely captured. The ability of the DWT to isolate localized discontinuities at multiple scales offers a stronger theoretical basis.

### 2. DWT in Ensemble-Based Feature Extraction

A study on **IOPscience** (May 2024) introduces an ensemble-based DNN model: This study introduces an ensemble-based Deep Neural Network model for detecting defects on steel surfaces, classifying steel surface conditions into six possible fault categories, namely, crazing, inclusion, rolled in, pitted surface, scratches, and patches. The images undergo preprocessing and extraction of features in spatial and frequency domains using image segmentation techniques such as GLDM, FFT, GLCM, texture analysis, and discrete wavelet transform (DWT).

### 3. Attention Mechanisms for Steel Surface Defect Detection

- **DSAT (Dynamic Sparse Attention Transformer)** — Published in *Scientific Reports* (August 2025): This research employs a dynamic attention window and multi-layer feature interaction to achieve a technical breakthrough in recognizing micro-defects on steel surfaces. It introduces a dynamic sparse attention mechanism which employs a two-stage feature focusing strategy. In the first stage, a coarse-grained screening mechanism filters out redundant background areas from the feature map. In the second stage, a fine-grained correlation analysis is conducted within the retained regions of interest.

- **Hybrid Attention Network** — Published in *Sensors* (2023): Defect detection in steel surface focuses on accurately identifying and precisely locating defects on the surface of steel materials. Methods of defect detection with deep learning have gained significant attention in research. Existing algorithms can achieve satisfactory results, but the accuracy still needs to be improved. Aiming at this issue, a hybrid attention network is proposed.

- **Multi-Scale Feature Fusion with Attention** — Published in *Scientific Reports* (April 2024): The multi-scale feature fusion (MSF) strategy is utilized to fuse shallow and deep features effectively and enrich detailed information relevant to target defects. The CSPLayer Res2Attention block (CRA block) residual module is introduced to reduce the loss of defect information during hierarchical transmission, thereby enhancing the extraction of fine-grained features.

### 4. Vision Transformers with Self-Attention

The self-attention mechanism in vision transformers enables the model to focus on relevant regions of the image, facilitating the identification of defects and distinguishing them from normal regions. The implementation of vision transformers for steel surface defect detection offers several advantages.

### 5. Lightweight Approaches (YOLO-based with Attention)

Gao et al. integrated the attention mechanism and weighted bi-directional feature pyramid network (BiFPN) into the YOLOv5 architecture, achieving good results. Subburaj et al. proposed the DBCW-YOLO model, which integrates attention mechanisms and enhanced feature extraction techniques to improve defect detection accuracy.

---

## Summary

Research in steel surface defect detection increasingly combines **DWT** (for multi-scale frequency analysis and edge detection) with **attention mechanisms** (for focusing on defect-relevant regions). The DWT excels at capturing localized discontinuities and enhancing subtle defect features, while attention mechanisms (self-attention, spatial attention, deformable attention, etc.) help models focus on the most informative regions. These approaches are commonly integrated into frameworks like YOLO, DETR, and Vision Transformers.

Here are the results for your search on **"wavelet YOLO defect detection 2025 2026"**. There is a wealth of recent research in this area:

---

## Key Papers & Findings

### 1. **WT-YOLO – Wavelet-Enhanced YOLO for Welding Defects (April 2025)**
This study integrates wavelet transform convolutions (WTConv) into YOLOv11n, creating WT-YOLO, to enhance defect detection in X-ray films. Wavelet transforms enable multi-resolution analysis, extracting both high-frequency and low-frequency features critical for detecting various welding defects. Trained on 7000 radiographic images, WT-YOLO achieved a 0.0212 increase in mAP75 and a 0.0479 improvement in precision compared to YOLOv11n. Compared to manual inspection, WT-YOLO achieved higher precision for cracks, undercut, slag inclusion, and porosity, with an inference speed 300 times faster than manual inspection.
- **Source:** MDPI Applied Sciences, [Link](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/15/8/4586)

### 2. **YOLOv11-WBD – Wavelet-Bidirectional Network for Metal Surface Defects (Sept 2025)**
YOLOv11-WBD is a wavelet-bidirectional network with dilated perception for robust metal surface defect detection, published in September 2025 in PLOS One.
- **Source:** PLOS One, [Link](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0331025)

### 3. **TriA-Mamba-YOLO – Steel Surface Defect Detection (March 2026)**
The authors propose TriA-Mamba-YOLO, a triple-awareness enhancement of the Mamba-YOLO framework for steel defect detection. The input samples are processed using a discrete wavelet transform, which reduces background noise and enhances the representation of defect features. Accurate and efficient surface defect detection is crucial for ensuring the structural integrity and quality of industrial steel products, but existing detectors still struggle with sub-pixel, low-contrast defects and extreme variations in defect scale and shape.
- **Source:** PeerJ Computer Science, [Link](https://peerj.com/articles/cs-3626/)

### 4. **CPD-YOLO – Chip Packaging Defect Detection (Jan 2026)**
CPD-YOLO addresses chip packaging defects using attention orthogonal fusion, wavelet transform, and YOLOv8, with chip packaging defect detection being critical for ensuring system operational stability.
- **Source:** Measurement Science and Technology, [Link](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/399217436)

### 5. **YOLO-WAD – Photovoltaic Module Small-Defect Detection (March 2025)**
The YOLO-WAD model, based on YOLOv10n, replaces C2f with C2f-WTConv (wavelet transform convolution) in the backbone network to enlarge the receptive field and better extract the features of small-target defects (hot spots) in photovoltaic modules.
- **Source:** PMC / MDPI, [Link](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11946470/)

### 6. **SAF-YOLO – Lyophilized Vial Defect Detection (Nov 2025)**
SAF-YOLO (Spectrum and Attention Fusion YOLO), built upon YOLOv11, is enhanced from the perspectives of spectrum perception and attention mechanisms for defect detection in lyophilized vials. For spectrum perception, the authors introduce the Wavelet-C3K2 module.
- **Source:** MDPI Electronics, [Link](https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/14/22/4526)

### 7. **CWSP-YOLO – Aerial Insulator Defect Detection (April 2026)**
This paper proposes an improved YOLOv11-based model integrated with multimodal data, featuring cross-modal collaboration, wavelet-optimized C3k2, channel attention, and PIoU v2-based dynamic gradient optimization for insulator defect detection. Experiments on a self-built dataset show it achieves 84.77% mean average precision, 94.53% accuracy, 82.38% recall, with 24 FPS meeting real-time requirements.
- **Source:** Nature Scientific Reports, [Link](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-026-40782-2)

### 8. **MWYOLO – Mamba-Enhanced YOLO with Multi-Frequency Attention (May 2026)**
A multi-scale wavelet attention (MWA) module is embedded into the backbone, leveraging wavelet-domain feature decomposition and dual attention to capture multi-frequency patterns, thereby improving sensitivity to fine-grained and subtle defect patterns. MWYOLO is an enhanced YOLO11-based detection framework tailored for accurate and efficient industrial inspection, addressing complex textures, diverse defect scales, and stringent real-time constraints.
- **Source:** AI Engineering, [Link](https://doi.org/10.3390/aieng1010003)

---

## Summary of Trends

The 2025–2026 research landscape shows a strong trend toward integrating **wavelet transforms** (especially discrete wavelet transforms and WTConv) into various YOLO architectures (YOLOv8, v10, v11, and Mamba-YOLO) to improve defect detection. Key application domains include **steel surfaces**, **welding X-rays**, **photovoltaic modules**, **chip packaging**, **insulators**, and **pharmaceutical packaging**. Wavelet-based modules are consistently used to enhance multi-scale feature extraction, reduce background noise, and improve detection of small or low-contrast defects.

## WAMF Module: What the Paper Actually Describes

Based solely on the article's content, here is what can be determined about WAMF's architecture:

### Step 1: Wavelet Decomposition

The module performs 2D discrete wavelet transform on the input image, producing four subbands. The paper states it decomposes using "low-pass filter and high-pass filter" applied in "horizontal and vertical directions." The outputs are:

- **LL**: "low-frequency features (e.g., smooth backgrounds and structural information)"
- **LH**: "horizontal high-frequency features (e.g., edges)"
- **HL**: "vertical high-frequency features"
- **HH**: "diagonal high-frequency features (typically dominated by noise)"

### Step 2: Concatenation and Dimensional Change

After wavelet decomposition, "the width and height dimensions of the original feature map are halved." The four subbands are concatenated, producing a feature map described as having shape involving the notation "4C" (the channel dimension quadruples). This inherently "achieves feature down sampling and increases channels."

### Step 3: Attention-Based Fusion (NOT Inverse DWT)

Critically, the module does **not** reconstruct via inverse DWT. Instead, "average pooling layers and multi-head attention mechanisms are connected in series to achieve adaptive fusion of multi-frequency features along the channel dimension." A gating mechanism then selectively retains valid high-frequency information while suppressing noise, producing an "integrated feature after dynamically suppressing noise and enhancing defects."

### What Is NOT Specified

The paper does not provide:
- The specific wavelet family used (e.g., Haar, Daubechies)
- Number of attention heads
- Exact gating mechanism formulation beyond Equations 2–3
- Whether per-subband attention is applied before or only after concatenation

### Summary

WAMF applies 2D DWT → concatenates the four subbands → applies average pooling + multi-head channel attention with gating for adaptive fusion. It does **not** use inverse DWT for reconstruction; instead, the fused representation feeds directly into subsequent backbone layers, partially replacing early convolution-based downsampling in YOLOv11.

Based on my search results, here is what I found about the **MWYOLO** framework and its **Multi-Scale Wavelet Attention (MWA)** module architecture:

## MWYOLO Overview

MWYOLO is an enhanced YOLO11-based detection framework tailored for accurate and efficient industrial inspection, addressing challenges like complex textures, diverse defect scales, and stringent real-time constraints in industrial surface defect detection.

The framework was recently published on MDPI (about 3 weeks ago) and includes three key innovations:

1. **C3k2-Mamba Spatial Fusion Block (C3k2-MSFB):** This integrates global contextual information with local structural cues via state-space modeling, enabling more discriminative representations of fine-grained texture variations.

2. **Multi-Scale Wavelet Attention (MWA) Module:** This is embedded into the backbone, leveraging wavelet-domain feature decomposition and dual attention to capture multi-frequency patterns, thereby improving sensitivity to fine-grained and subtle defect patterns.

3. **Inner-CIoU Loss:** An Inner-CIoU loss is developed to emphasize interior geometric alignment during bounding-box regression, offering more stable optimization.

## MWA Module Architecture Details

### DWT Subband Decomposition
Unlike conventional convolutional layers that operate solely in the spatial domain, MWA leverages the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to decompose input features into complementary sub-bands. Given an input feature map, the DWT produces sub-bands where high-frequency components (LH, HL, HH) capture horizontal, vertical, and diagonal edge details.

### Multi-Kernel Convolutions on Subbands
To enrich the receptive field, multi-kernel convolutions are applied to each sub-band and then fused. Smaller kernels (e.g., k=3) are more effective for processing high-frequency detail sub-bands to preserve sharp defect boundaries, while larger kernels (e.g., k=5) are better suited for the low-frequency sub-band to capture broader contextual information.

### Dual-Path Architecture (Multi-Scale Wavelet + Dual Attention)
The transformed representations are fed into two parallel branches: (1) a **multi-scale wavelet branch**, which employs wavelet-domain convolutions with varying kernel sizes to capture structural patterns across different scales; and (2) a **dual-attention branch**, which enhances feature representations by jointly modeling inter-channel dependencies and intra-feature spatial saliency. This architecture ensures that the attention mechanism operates on frequency-enriched features, thereby enabling more informed and discriminative feature weighting.

### Receptive Field Enhancement
Performing convolution in the wavelet domain effectively enlarges the receptive field, enabling the network to simultaneously capture both fine-grained and broader contextual features.

### Output Integration
The forward processing involves convolution, batch normalization, and activation steps. Following the dual-path processing, the outputs from both processing branches are subsequently integrated.

## Related Context on Inverse DWT

While the search results don't provide full implementation details of the inverse DWT step within MWA specifically, the general pattern in wavelet-based attention modules is well-established: the output is reconstructed through inverse DWT, enabling selective frequency suppression or enhancement. This is consistent with how wavelet transform and the inverse wavelet transform are substituted for down-sampling and up-sampling so feature maps from the wavelet transform and convolutions contain different frequencies and scales.

The full paper is available at: **https://www.mdpi.com/3042-8831/1/1/3** (published in MDPI's AI Engineering journal).

content.text:
Here is my assessment based on thorough searching across all five queries.

---

**Verdict: Your exact combination is NOT published, but the neighborhood is crowded. Several very recent papers are close.**

**Closest matches (ranked by proximity to your design):**

1. **MWYOLO** (MDPI AI Engineering, May 2026) -- Multi-Scale Wavelet Attention (MWA) module embedded in YOLO11 backbone. Applies DWT to decompose features into subbands, runs multi-kernel convolutions per subband, then feeds into a dual-attention branch (channel + spatial). This is for *general* industrial surface defects, not steel-specific. **Very close** -- but uses multi-kernel convolutions on subbands rather than pure per-subband channel attention, and does not explicitly use inverse DWT for reconstruction.

2. **YOLOv11-WBD** (PLOS One, Sept 2025) -- Wavelet-Attentive Multiband Fusion Module (WAMF) inside YOLOv11. Applies 2D DWT, concatenates 4 subbands (LL/LH/HL/HH), then applies average pooling + multi-head channel attention with gating. **Does NOT reconstruct via inverse DWT** -- the fused representation feeds forward directly. Targets metal surface defects.

3. **HDSA-YOLO** (ResearchGate, Dec 2025) -- Multi-scale Haar wavelet fusion + Deformable-Swin integration for steel defect detection. Wavelet decomposition used for downsampling/feature extraction, but attention is Swin-based spatial attention, not per-subband channel attention.

4. **TriA-Mamba-YOLO** (PeerJ CS, Mar 2026) -- Uses DWT as preprocessing on input images (not on intermediate feature maps) plus Selective Fusion Attention for steel defects. Different architecture from yours.

5. **HDFA-Net** (ScienceDirect, Nov 2024) -- Decoupled frequency attention at channel+spatial levels for steel defects. Uses frequency-domain decomposition for attention weighting but is not YOLO-based and does not use DWT specifically.

**What distinguishes your proposed module:**
Your design -- 2D DWT on intermediate YOLO feature maps, independent per-subband channel attention (not concatenate-then-attend), followed by inverse DWT reconstruction -- has no exact published match. The key differentiators are (a) per-subband channel attention applied *before* fusion, and (b) inverse DWT reconstruction to maintain spatial resolution. Existing work either concatenates subbands then attends, skips inverse DWT, applies wavelets only to input images, or uses spatial/Swin attention instead of channel attention.

**Conclusion:** The idea is novel in its specific formulation, but you are operating in a very active space (at least 6 relevant papers from 2025-2026). You should cite MWYOLO and YOLOv11-WBD as closest related work and clearly articulate the per-subband attention + inverse DWT reconstruction as your contribution.

Sources:
- [MWYOLO (MDPI 2026)](https://www.mdpi.com/3042-8831/1/1/3)
- [YOLOv11-WBD (PLOS One 2025)](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0331025)
- [HDSA-YOLO (ResearchGate 2025)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/398267055_HDSA-YOLO_a_steel_surface_defect_detection_network_via_multi-scale_haar_wavelet_fusion_and_deformable-swin_integration)
- [TriA-Mamba-YOLO (PeerJ 2026)](https://peerj.com/articles/cs-3626/)
- [HDFA-Net (ScienceDirect 2024)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0263224124021407)
- [Wavelet upsampling + frequency attention (Taylor & Francis 2025)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10589759.2025.2566787)
